does: This paper addresses the problem of sycophancy in large language models by reframing it as a reasoning optimization challenge rather than an output alignment issue. The authors introduce SMART, a two-stage framework that combines uncertainty-aware adaptive Monte Carlo tree search (UA-MCTS) with reinforcement learning. UA-MCTS dynamically adjusts model exploration based on state-level uncertainty to collect diverse, high-quality reasoning trajectories with both stepwise progress and outcome rewards. The framework then applies progress-based reinforcement learning to fine-tune the model using these trajectories and reward signals. Experimental results demonstrate that SMART substantially reduces sycophantic behavior—where models agree with incorrect user information—while maintaining strong performance on out-of-distribution tasks, suggesting the approach learns more robust reasoning patterns rather than surface-level agreement behaviors.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research addressing alignment failures in large language models, particularly the sycophancy problem identified in recent studies. It extends prior work on using reinforcement learning for model improvement and combines this with Monte Carlo tree search methods that have proven effective in reasoning tasks. The paper builds on uncertainty-aware decision-making approaches from Bayesian optimization and active learning literature, adapting these to LLM reasoning. It connects to broader work on reward modeling and trajectory-based learning, as well as research examining how models can be steered toward principled reasoning rather than user-pleasing outputs. The emphasis on reasoning trajectories rather than just final outputs aligns with recent focus on process-based versus outcome-based reward models in AI alignment.
enables: This work opens several future research directions for improving LLM reliability and alignment. The uncertainty-aware adaptive reasoning approach could be extended to other systematic behavioral biases beyond sycophancy, such as confidence calibration and hallucination reduction. The framework may enable development of more robust evaluation metrics that assess reasoning quality rather than output agreement. Future work could explore how trajectory-based learning scales to longer reasoning tasks or more complex domains. The approach potentially enables safer deployment of LLMs in high-stakes settings by ensuring models reason independently from user bias. Additionally, this methodology could inform better training procedures for instilling intellectual independence in models, and may facilitate better human-AI collaboration where models can confidently contradict incorrect user assumptions.
